item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Photo updated for licensing reasons to this one by Dejan Krsmanovic.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) due to it being all rights reserved. As such, the image have been removed.

EDIT: The image has been replaced with a cc-compliant alternative.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded: Y'all keep seeing the links I don't introduce. Love it, and consider it done.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired. Edit already made by author.
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Proposed Edits
- Proposed Edits

A crosslink is being proposed to link SCP-2458 to the On Mount Golgotha hub page, as the article in question references the story, and the hub page links back to the article. In addition, the author of the article is also the author of the hub page.

Editing this:

May we all choose penitence as St. Dismas did on Mount Golgotha.

To this:

May we all choose penitence as St. Dismas did on Mount Golgotha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept of a Creator's Explanatory Note In The Addendum that, instead of telling us the reasoning behind the object's anomalous effects, shows us the idea the creator had in mind when they set out… and leaves us to compare this to the actual effects, and conclude that the creator got it _totally wrong._ Not sure I've seen that before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Skincube has created art for SCP-2458.

Check out the rest of their stuff, too!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't really work out for me. It's selling itself as an improvement on 012 (which is persistently derided for its blandness) but this really isn't all that better in terms in blandness (though it is obviously markedly better in terms of tone). To be honest these effects are the most obvious effects I can think of for a cello (forces listeners to do X) and everything else just adds additional layers of bland predictability (it kills you by forcing you to self-harm, recovered from an abandoned psychiatric hospital, cryptic note from the object's creator hinting at its purpose, none-too-subtle arc words spelling the connection out). I just really don't see anything all that compelling here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i really like it when this happens.

it gives the implications, that all skips that are contained and researched long enough will turn out to have a much bigger impact and harsher effect that believed at first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. That's an admirable goal, and reminiscent of what's happened with 076 and 682, but I'd rather just see it downvoted to oblivion, -ARC'd, and something better/a rewrite put in its place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I get you, MacLeod. That's why I figured if we can't just rewrite that article into something worthwhile, let's build around it, cover it with prettier walls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate SCP-012, like, so much. It is probably the worst article on the site that is still successful. Anything that further cements it into the site angers me to no end. I am, therefore, displeased with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is predictable in ways others have mentioned, although I like the 012 link. No vote, although it could be improved after the contest.

item.title: On Mount Golgotha
item.content:encoded: Replying to myself, sorry… Found your brainstorm thread and bookmarked it, WrongJohnSilver! Shall read and participate ASAP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• Instrument that compels you to dance/move/do stuff feels extremely predictable. I don't think I'd downvote for that on its own, but taken together with the rest of the elements, this characterizes the article's feel of rote execution.
  • Hey look, it kills you. Again, maybe not worth the downvote on its own, but another piece of a dreary landscape I've seen many times before.
  • I don't like the expungement of data for gore. In-universe, these are scientific documents that would be required to be unflinching in detail. Out-of-universe, it just seems silly. If the gore is so over-the-top that you feel the need to leave it to the reader or refrain from writing it because it sounds goofy or what have you, do you really need to have it in the article at all?
  • Convenient note at the end. Everyone is perpetually leaving cryptic notes hinting around at why they went to their anomalous workshop and made whatever thing it is that the Foundation needs to contain. That's a device that I will downvote for on its own, however I don't think I've ever seen an article where that was the only problem.

Too many major issues to refrain from downvoting this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And some excellent dancing!

I'll have a look at your brainstorm thread, but after the contest. I'm already spending far too much time on this website right now! Need to read all the SC, and comment if I feel the need to express myself, and I'm only about half-way through… 8-[

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry you felt the need to downvote.

+ show block
- hide block

The idea was that D-7294 was the one who controlled the listeners into harming themselves and each other. The issue is that the control is tied directly to what is being played. If you play regular music (even most of Shostakovich), the set rhythm and tempo and scale means that only a small subset of action are performed. Play Twinkle Twinkle Little Star, and you'll only get a little head bobbing. More complex music can get you more complex movement, like Bach, but even then, if you really want to control someone's actions, you have to play the right notes at the right time, musicality be damned. D-7294 is being kept around because he's figured out how to use SCP-2458 to its greatest effect.

As to the 012 allusion, I wanted it obscured, mainly so that I couldn't rely on it to strengthen the piece. The article has to stand on its own. The religious references? Well… I feel that given the provenance of 012, the other anomalous instruments, Bach's famous ecclesiastical works, and the historic culture of northern and central Italy, there is going to be a Christian - and in particular Catholic - theme throughout.

I'm actually considering starting to write tales about this, SCP-012, and other anomalous musical instruments. Have you seen my brainstorm thread on On Mount Golgotha? I'd welcome your input and contributions.

And dang, that link was some good music!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the first version very much, without the logs, except the description of the anomalous effects.

+ show block
– hide block

I can only copy-paste what I wrote in the comments re: your sandbox:

The initial 'compulsion' is rather weak, IMO, and doesn't have much to do with the final effect of melophobia. Besides, who has never nodded in rhythm and hummed along when hearing a good piece? You could probably do without this paragraph altogether, which would be good for your word count! ;>
Finally, I somehow would like the origin of the SCP to be linked more closely to its effect. Right now, I can't imagine why this kind and generous gift to a hospital would induce melophobia, screaming and ear-clawing… Either establish a link through one or two clues, or change the origin? What say you?

Now, I see that you 'cleaned up' the whole text so it would be shorter and more flowing, which is good, and could have allowed you some liberty for adding things.
But I didn't like the experiment logs, especially the last one, and still don't. They ruin everything for me.
You explain what you wanted to do - D-7294 figuring out what the cello does and having some fun - but IMO, it's not well-done.
Does D-7294 take control of the listeners, or does he just make them lose control of themselves?
Is the ultimate anomalous effect of the cello making listeners self-harm? But then why did two D-class attack each other? Not clear.
The allusion to SCP-012 is not clear in-text, and the references to religion (St-Dismas, Golgotha, praying and chanting the Kyrie in log #2) feel out of place.

Sorry, WrongJohnSilver - downvote from me. This skip feels constricted within 500 words - perhaps expand after the contest, to be able to say/imply more?

Fun facts:
- Shostakovitch's "music" (!) does tend to make me lose control too - but it gives me Shostakovitchophobia, not melophobia!
- I wonder what would happen if this cello was used in place of the contrabass in this piece: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mUbweBA5fh0 (Lindy hop scene, music + dancing, from Hellzapoppin') = one of the wildest pieces of music I've EVER heard!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had no idea one (or rather, two) could make so much noise with two cellos! ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like its effects are fairly expected, and the kill-you-to-death part in the last test is just ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is awesome. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adding this strictly for fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the way the world ends, not with a bang but to the tune of an orchestra.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The implication is that SCP-012 was designed to be played on this thing, and the score's seeming dissonance and disregard for musical convention is intentional; it's not trying to be music, it's some sort of insidious sequence of commands masquerading as music, just waiting to be played on this cello so those listening can do whatever horrifying thing it was designed to get them to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens if you try to play SCP-012 on this?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suppose I can see that. It wasn't the intention, but I'll accept it.

An earlier log (that was too long) included Stravinsky's Rite of Spring as well, where some of the more nuances effects became clear.

And, well, Pachelbel's Canon in D would likely not do anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I literally snorted when I got to the part about Shostakovich, but the ending note was eerily similar to what I did for SCP-1758, so I didn't quite feel comfortable upvoting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good! +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • I am mildly intrigued by the reference to 012, which is good since I was otherwise sceptical to this SCP.
  • I actually like the initial 2 tests for not being a D-class snuff fest.

Cons:

  • And then… there's the last test that actually did that…

Verdict: Upvote (tentatively)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna be thinking about the SCP-012 connection for a while now. Good work. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Repeating what I said in the critique thread; the idea of music being actively malicious terrifies the absolute shit out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Photo by Tiffany Bailey.

Many, many thanks to sirpudding, Eta Carinae, MrWrong, psul, Scantron, Thekillerax, and Decibelles for their time and critique!
